content:
First, factor 391=17·23.  For any pair (a,b) define
   R = lcm(a,b)/lcm(a,391).  
Writing each in terms of prime‐exponents one sees that “new’’ primes in b (primes p∉{17,23}) contribute factors p^{v_p(b)–v_p(a)}, while missing 17 or 23 (i.e. v_{17}(a)=0 or v_{23}(a)=0) introduce denominators 17 or 23.  To make R>1 we want

   (product of new‐prime powers of b)   >   (product of the primes in {17,23} that do NOT divide a).

We cannot make a multiple of 391 (that would force a≥391), so the best we can do is choose a divisible by exactly one of 17,23 to reduce the “missing‐product’’ to 17 or 23.  To minimize b we pick the smaller of these, namely 17.  Hence we need

   (new‐prime product of b)  > 17.

Scanning b from 2 upward, the first time ∏_{p|b, p∉{17,23}} p^{v_p(b)} exceeds 17 is at b=18, since for b=18=2·3^2 the new‐prime product is 2·3^2=18>17.  For example take a=23 (so lcm(23,391)=391) and b=18 gives lcm(23,18)=414>391.  Hence the minimum possible b is

\boxed{18}.

message.content:
First, factor 391=17·23.  For any pair (a,b) define
   R = lcm(a,b)/lcm(a,391).  
Writing each in terms of prime‐exponents one sees that “new’’ primes in b (primes p∉{17,23}) contribute factors p^{v_p(b)–v_p(a)}, while missing 17 or 23 (i.e. v_{17}(a)=0 or v_{23}(a)=0) introduce denominators 17 or 23.  To make R>1 we want

   (product of new‐prime powers of b)   >   (product of the primes in {17,23} that do NOT divide a).

We cannot make a multiple of 391 (that would force a≥391), so the best we can do is choose a divisible by exactly one of 17,23 to reduce the “missing‐product’’ to 17 or 23.  To minimize b we pick the smaller of these, namely 17.  Hence we need

   (new‐prime product of b)  > 17.

Scanning b from 2 upward, the first time ∏_{p|b, p∉{17,23}} p^{v_p(b)} exceeds 17 is at b=18, since for b=18=2·3^2 the new‐prime product is 2·3^2=18>17.  For example take a=23 (so lcm(23,391)=391) and b=18 gives lcm(23,18)=414>391.  Hence the minimum possible b is

\boxed{18}.
